title: When AI Writes the Solutions, Who Writes the Questions?
text:
Paper meeting · ADRS · systems research


When AI Writes the Solutions, Who Writes the Questions?


A long-form reading guide for Barbarians at the Gate: How AI is Upending Systems Research. Figures are the anchor, but the surrounding text gives enough context for discussion.


Prepared by Han-Yin Chang · NTHU CS



Working thesis. ADRS does not make research automatic. It moves the bottleneck from manually writing every solution to defining the problem, constructing the evaluator, and deciding whether a result contains real insight.



workflow ADRS loop cases transaction scheduling failure modes limits my workflow collaboration references







What the paper is really about


The paper’s scope is narrower than “AI writes a whole systems paper.” It argues that systems research is unusually compatible with AI-driven solution discovery because many systems problems have executable verification: a candidate can be placed in a simulator or real system, run on workloads, and measured against a baseline.[P]


Marc Brooker makes the same point from a software-engineering angle: the next decade may be shaped by how well we build reliable verifiers, and testing becomes central when AI can optimize rapidly against a target.[B]



LLMs generate candidate mutations. The evaluator performs natural selection.









Research workflow: what ADRS does and does not automate


The paper decomposes systems research into five stages: problem formulation, evaluation framework, solution, evaluation, and paper write-up. ADRS targets only the middle loop: solution and evaluation.[P]






Figure 1. ADRS targets the Solution and Evaluation stages, not the whole research process.





This boundary matters. The paper is not claiming that problem selection, benchmark design, interpretation, or paper writing are solved. If anything, those parts become more important because the inner loop becomes cheaper.








Why this inner loop is tempting to automate


The paper motivates ADRS by arguing that algorithm design and evaluation consume a large fraction of systems research time. The exact number should not be overread; the survey is small. But the direction is plausible: a lot of research time is spent trying candidate methods and measuring whether they work.






Figure 2. Algorithm Design and Evaluation together exceed 40% in the paper’s survey.





The hidden cost is important. The reported automation time does not fully capture human effort in refining prompts, constructing evaluators, debugging the setup, or deciding what the result means. This is where a critical reading should not become marketing.








The ADRS loop


Figure 3 is the central figure. A prompt generator packages the problem and context; a solution generator uses LLMs to produce candidates; an evaluator scores them; storage records the trajectory; and a solution selector chooses what to keep exploring.[P]






Figure 3. The ADRS loop is generate → evaluate → store → select, with optional human outer-loop guidance.





The most important object in this diagram is not the LLM. It is the evaluator. Without a good evaluator, the loop merely produces fluent-looking artifacts. With a good evaluator, the loop becomes a search process over executable solutions.



If the evaluator is wrong, the loop does not become slower. It becomes confidently wrong faster.









The case studies: useful evidence, not a clean leaderboard


Table 1 reports 11 tasks across systems, databases, networking, and ML systems. It is useful because it shows that the ADRS pattern can be applied in several places. But I would not present it as a clean leaderboard: the paper notes that different students ran the studies in parallel with different configurations and without extensive ablations.[P]






Table 1. Evidence map, not a universal proof that “AI beats SOTA.”





Before showing before/after code, each case needs a short problem statement. Otherwise the audience only sees code snippets without knowing what the algorithm is trying to optimize.





Can’t Be Late: deadline scheduling with unreliable cheap spot instances


The system wants to run jobs cheaply using spot instances, but spot instances can disappear. The policy must save cost while still meeting deadlines. The evaluator checks deadline validity and cost savings over traces.




MoE Expert Placement: balance expert load across GPUs


The system must place expert replicas across GPUs so that token load is balanced. The reported improvement comes from replacing Python-loop placement with tensorized zigzag placement.




LLM-SQL: reorder table rows and fields for KV-cache reuse


SQL-style analytics invokes LLM inference row by row. Reordering rows and fields can increase shared prompt prefixes, improving prefix cache hit rate. The evolved solution preserves hit rate while reducing reordering runtime.




Transaction Scheduling: reorder transactions to reduce conflicts


Transaction conflicts create bottlenecks. The goal is to schedule transactions so the makespan is minimized. This case is especially useful because online ADRS only rediscovers SOTA, while offline ADRS explores a new heavier search variant.
